questions.question: What is the difference between `print` and `return` in a function?

answers.text: return hands a value back to the caller to use; print only displays it
return displays text; print hands a value back

questions.feedback: return produces a usable value (and ends the function). print just shows text and hands back None.

questions.question: Given `def greet(name, greeting="Hello"):`, which call is INVALID?
questions.feedback: name has no default, so it must be supplied. greet(greeting="Hi") omits name and errors.

questions.question: Which import lets you call `sqrt(16)` directly (without a prefix)?
questions.feedback: `from math import sqrt` brings the name in directly. `import math` needs math.sqrt(...). The third option isn't valid syntax.

questions.question: Which are good practices with function arguments?

answers.text: Use keyword arguments to make a call clearer
Put parameters with defaults after those without
Always prefer *args/**kwargs over named parameters in your own code

questions.feedback: Keyword args aid clarity, defaults go last, and mutable defaults are a trap. Explicit named params are usually clearer than *args/**kwargs in your own early code.
